title: Writing - Part 1: Plastic Pollution
description: Your class has listened to a discussion on the global plastic pollution crisis and the measures required to tackle the growing accumulation of plastic waste in natural environments.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the aspects in your notes. You should explain which aspect you consider most important in tackling plastic pollution, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: Manufacturers, not consumers, are primarily responsible for plastic pollution.
Consumers choosing sustainable products creates real pressure on manufacturers.
Without global agreements, plastic waste simply moves to poorer nations.

structure.introduction: Introduce the plastic pollution crisis and state the two aspects you will discuss.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Examine the first aspect (e.g. corporate responsibility), considering the extent to which industry must drive change.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Discuss the second aspect (e.g. international regulation), explaining why you view it as the most critical factor.
structure.conclusion: Summarise your arguments and make a clear recommendation about where priority action is needed.

content.modelAnswer:
Plastic pollution has reached crisis proportions, with an estimated eight million tonnes of plastic entering the world's oceans annually. Addressing this catastrophe requires action on multiple fronts, but two considerations stand out as particularly central: corporate responsibility and the role of international regulation.

Corporations that produce and sell goods in single-use plastic packaging are arguably the primary architects of this crisis. The design choices made in factory boardrooms — to prioritise cheap, lightweight plastics over sustainable alternatives — have consequences that ultimately fall upon ecosystems and communities that had no part in those decisions. Encouragingly, consumer pressure and reputational risk have prompted some manufacturers to commit to reducing plastic packaging, yet voluntary pledges in the absence of legal obligation have consistently proven insufficient. Genuine corporate accountability requires enforceable targets and meaningful penalties for non-compliance.

However, the most transformative intervention would be a robust framework of international regulation. Plastic pollution is inherently a transboundary problem: waste generated in one country contaminates the coastlines of another, and the global nature of supply chains means that national legislation alone cannot address the root causes. A binding international treaty — modelled on the Paris Agreement — that mandates production limits, sets standardised recycling requirements and prohibits the export of plastic waste to nations lacking adequate processing facilities would provide the systemic framework that voluntary commitments and piecemeal national laws cannot.

In conclusion, while corporate accountability is essential, only coordinated international governance can deliver the scale of change that the plastic pollution crisis demands.
